title: From blue‑skies science to blue-chip technologies
text:

TRACEGET houses equipment worth around €14 million to support advanced and complex experiments.Credit: Innovation Agency LithuaniaWhen Virginijus Šikšnys started working on the anti-viral defences of bacteria, he never imagined his research would contribute to a game-changing medical treatment. Šikšnys, a biochemist at the Life Sciences Center at Vilnius University in Lithuania, was one of the scientists who pioneered CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing1, which is now used to treat patients with sickle cell anaemia, a common genetic disease. Meeting one of these patients was a very personal moment for the Lithuanian scientist. “When you're doing research, you don't think about these things,” says Šikšnys. "But when you see it developed into a technology to help people — that makes a huge difference." Virginijus Šikšnys is one of the CRISPR-Cas9 pioneers and is spearheading the creation of TRACEGET to inspire future breakthroughs.Credit: Vilnius University, Life Sciences CenterSeeing the impact of basic research when translated into applications, Šikšnys is spearheading the creation of an institution in Vilnius: the Translational Center for Gene Technologies, or TRACEGET. The city has a long history of world-class biotechnology research: it played a key role in the recombinant DNA revolution of the 1970s and became a leading producer of molecular biology enzymes. Its excellence continues today, particularly in gene editing and multi-omics. “A lot of cool research is happening in academic labs at Vilnius University," says Šikšnys. But there was no centralized facility to turn these discoveries into new technologies and treatments. In one building, TRACEGET will have eight core facilities, including microfluidics, gene editing, single-cell sequencing and mass spectrometry, supported by some €14 million of equipment. The centre is the focus of Lithuania's Innovations for Health mission, part of a €88.34 million national programme, funded through the EU's Recovery and Resilience Plan and Lithuania’s state budget. It was initiated by the Ministry of Economy and Innovation and the Ministry of Education, Science and Sport, and coordinated by Innovation Agency Lithuania. Access for science The centre is already demonstrating its value, says biotechnologist Linas Mažutis. "Previously, if you wanted access to a flow cytometer you needed to drive to another part of the city," he says. "Now everything is in one place. That saves time, but also means we can plan more complex experiments." Biotechnologist Linas Mažutis is using TRACEGET to explore new uses of semi-permeable capsules.Credit: Linas MažutisWorking within TRACEGET, Mažutis's team is developing new applications for a technology they have created, known as semi-permeable capsules. These are microscopic liquid droplets surrounded by a very thin envelope. “Inside that liquid compartment you can have cells, bacteria, viruses, DNA and so on,” says Mažutis. The envelope lets reagents pass through while keeping the contents encapsulated, offering advantages over existing approaches, which only allow one experimental intervention at a time. "Using capsules, you can conduct multi-step workflows that you simply cannot do using droplet microfluidics," says Mažutis, who is also a researcher at the Life Sciences Center, Vilnius University. In 2025, Mažutis’s team reported using the technology to discover how leukaemia cells disrupt the immune system, opening avenues for drug development2. The capsules have now been licensed to Atrandi Biosciences, a Vilnius-based company, co-founded by Mažutis originally as Droplet Genomics. It joins Caszyme, co-founded in 2017 by Šikšnys to develop and commercialize CRISPR-based technologies, as part of the city's blooming biotech ecosystem. Lithuania's life sciences sector is among the fastest-growing in Europe, with a national ambition to reach 5% of GDP by 2030. TRACEGET will help to realise that goal, says Šikšnys, not only by providing the facilities but also the environment to cultivate a translational mindset. "I hope,” he says, “that this centre will allow the scientists who are doing really cool curiosity-driven research to think a bit more about technology development and applications."
